evidence: Both target files expose contract surface through package-level symbols and helper-based derivation (`protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`) without an explicit schema/validation step at registration boundaries.

The helper in `protocol_contracts.go` hard-codes a dotted-string contract-token contract (`strings.Split(contractID, ".")` and taking the final segment), so cross-module token parsing behavior is coupled to a string convention not enforced at compile-time.

The package-level action token bindings are mutable `var` values (`ActionUploadOpenEndpoint`, `ActionUploadReset`, `ActionC2CConnect`), which increases cross-module coupling risk if external code mutates them.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: A stronger contract-definition boundary (explicit action token fields/typed metadata + immutability) would better enforce cross-module compatibility and prevent subtle boundary drift.

evidence: In `goseg/defaults/defaults.go`, `SysConfig(basePath string)` ignores its input and always sets `DockerData: DockerData("basePath")`, so callers cannot influence the configured docker data path through the API surface.

`goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go` and `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` independently define an `adaptUrbitSectionMutation` adapter with the same type-erasing wrapper pattern for section mutation calls, indicating repeated ad-hoc abstraction instead of a single shared seam.

Across `goseg/defaults/defaults.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, dependency wiring remains mostly package-local and wrapper-heavy rather than a consistent reusable abstraction boundary, increasing indirection and drift risk.

The batch docs list strict contract-gate expectations in `README.md`, but the contract surfaces here (`protocol_contracts.go`/`startram_contracts.go`) expose non-trivial mapping logic (`actionTokenFromContractID`) without in-scope regression assertions in this area.

Governance text references path-aware checks and test scripts, but mapping invariants for token extraction/metadata linkage are not represented as executable local assertions on these files themselves.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Add focused contract tests for catalog integrity (ID, namespace, action token, and metadata binding) adjacent to the contract definitions so governance is enforced by compile-time and unit-test signals, not only CI scripts.

evidence: goseg/config/urbit.go and goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go both ship their own `adaptUrbitSectionMutation` implementations instead of sharing one utility.

Urbit config sections are modeled twice (`UrbitConfigSection`) in `goseg/config/urbit.go` (typed int enum) and `goseg/structs/configs.go` (typed string enum), requiring coordinated manual updates across packages.

The Startram reconcile path in `goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go` mixes discovery, service registration, and persistence decisions inside one function, making mutation policy hard to reason about and extend.

findings.summary: Contract token extraction is implicit and tied to dot-delimited IDs, creating fragile cross-module coupling.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` derives action tokens via `actionTokenFromContractID`, which parses `ContractID` by splitting on `.` and using the last segment, so token identity semantics are encoded as string-shape conventions rather than explicit API contracts.

`startram_contracts.go` uses a different declaration style (embedded constants and literal descriptor fields) and does not participate in explicit, typed token definitions, increasing asymmetry in contract registration assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Replace implicit parsing with explicit token constants per contract (for example `const ActionUploadOpenEndpoint ActionToken = "open-endpoint"`) and keep catalog construction using those explicit values; add a small validator to fail fast when contract IDs do not match the expected namespace/action shape.

findings.summary: Exported mutable token variables expose the contract registry to accidental cross-package mutation.
evidence: Protocol contract tokens are declared as exported `var` values in `protocol_contracts.go`, which can be reassigned by external packages because they are not read-only contract constants.
The same contract package is intended as a boundary/API surface for protocol identifiers, so mutable public vars broaden the potential blast radius for coupling bugs.
findings.suggestion: Make contract tokens immutable at the public API boundary by using `const` or unexported storage with exported accessors, and avoid exposing mutable variables from contract modules.

findings.summary: Config factory exposes `basePath` but silently hard-codes docker data to the literal "basePath", breaking the abstraction contract.
evidence: `goseg/defaults/defaults.go` `SysConfig(basePath string)` assigns `DockerData: DockerData("basePath")`, while the `basePath` argument is never used.
Callers in higher-level setup (e.g. startup wiring in `goseg/main.go`) pass a caller-derived path expecting it to influence generated defaults.
findings.suggestion: Use the `basePath` argument directly when constructing defaults (or rename/remove parameter if unused): `DockerData: DockerData(basePath)`; add a unit test that asserts caller-supplied path is preserved.

findings.summary: Identical urbit section mutation adapter logic is duplicated in multiple packages, creating fragile copy-paste abstractions.
evidence: Both files implement `adaptUrbitSectionMutation[T any](mutateFn func(*T) error) func(any) error` using equivalent type assertion and delegated mutation behavior.
The duplicated helper encodes the same abstraction seam in separate packages rather than centralizing in one shared utility.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single shared generic adapter utility in a small internal package used by both runtime construction paths, and update both callers; add a focused test table for the shared function to prevent divergence.

findings.summary: Action token is computed by parsing contract IDs, coupling runtime semantics to string format
evidence: `actionTokenFromContractID` splits `ContractID` by "." and uses the last segment as the action token.
This makes token identity depend on string encoding convention rather than explicit contract API declarations.
findings.suggestion: Replace implicit parsing with explicit per-contract `ActionToken` constants and wire `contractCatalogSpec` entries to those constants directly. Keep string parsing only in migration/compatibility adapter code with hard failure when format assumptions change.

findings.summary: Protocol/StarTram contract mappings lack local regression tests for catalog consistency
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines catalog specs and derived token extraction logic, but no inline assertions or test-adjacent coverage are visible in the reviewed contract surfaces.
`README.md` documents strict contract-gate expectations but that expectation is not mirrored as explicit tests tied to these mapping functions.
findings.suggestion: Add `protocol_contracts_test.go` and `startram_contracts_test.go` with table-driven tests for ID, namespace, action token, and descriptor/metadata wiring, plus a regression test that rejects malformed or unexpected contract ID shapes.

findings.summary: Duplicate urbit section mutation adapters are duplicated across config and reconcile packages.
evidence: `config/urbit.go` defines `adaptUrbitSectionMutation[T any]` as a package-private generic section adapter.
`rectify/rectify_services.go` redefines the same generic adapter function with identical behavior and same error string pattern.
This helper is only kept in sync by convention, so behavior changes in one location are easy to miss in the other.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single shared typed helper (for example in an internal package) and use it from both call sites; keep section-specific wrappers in each package if needed but avoid duplicated casting/validation logic.

findings.summary: Urbit section enums are defined in two separate type systems, creating drift-prone duplication.
evidence: `goseg/config/urbit.go` declares `type UrbitConfigSection int` with constants `{Runtime, Network, Schedule, ...}`.
`goseg/structs/configs.go` separately declares `type UrbitConfigSection string` with the same domain labels and string values.
Any new section or semantic rename requires two independent updates and tests currently do not enforce cross-type parity.
findings.suggestion: Define a canonical `UrbitConfigSection` representation once (or shared generated mapping) and consume it across config runtime + serialized models, with a compile-time or startup check that enforces the canonical section set.

findings.summary: Startram retrieve reconciliation mixes discovery, service creation, and persistence orchestration in one oversized control path.
evidence: `StartramRetrieveReconciler.Reconcile` first scans the same `Subdomains` list to infer existing service presence, then rescans it for web/network reconciliation.
The function also performs config loading/persistence decisions (`updateWebConfig`, `updateNetworkConfig`) and publishes registration transitions in-line with low-level per-remote branching.
This broad responsibility boundary makes it difficult to add service-type-specific reconciliation without expanding a high-coupling function.
findings.suggestion: Split `Reconcile` into explicit phases: collect expected/existing subdomain index, compute a diff, then apply persistence and transition publication through dedicated coordinators so each concern (discovery, decision, mutation, side effects) is independently testable.
